title: Add tier classification at task creation
context:
Currently task.model_tier is null when the orchestrator picks up tasks. This means ModelChooser has to guess the tier at execution time. We want to classify once at creation. See design doc at docs/tier-classification-and-routing-overhaul.md.

Create app/services/task_tier.py with an async classify_task_tier(task, db) -> str function. Logic (first match wins):
1. If task.model_tier already set -> return it (caller override)
2. Project hard minimums: if task.project_id contains 'lobs-server', 'lobs-mission-control', or 'lobs-mobile' -> minimum is 'standard'
3. LLM classification: POST to http://localhost:1234/v1/chat/completions (LM Studio OpenAI-compat) with 2s timeout. Extract single word: small|standard|strong. On timeout/error -> default 'standard'
4. Default: 'standard'

Hook into create_task() in app/routers/tasks.py: after 'await db.flush()' and 'await db.refresh(db_task)', call classify_task_tier(db_task, db), then set db_task.model_tier = result, then call db.commit().

Also check app/schemas.py TaskCreate: ensure model_tier field is Optional[str] = None. If it already accepts it, no change needed.

LM Studio prompt (pass as user message, system message should be empty):
"Classify this software task into one tier:\n- small: boilerplate, docs, config, experiments, scripts, simple bug fixes\n- standard: production features, multi-file changes, API endpoints, refactors, research\n- strong: architecture, security, complex debugging, system design, DB migrations\n\nTask title: {title}\nProject: {project_id}\nAgent: {agent}\nNotes (first 500 chars): {notes[:500]}\n\nReply with exactly one word: small, standard, or strong."
acceptance:
1. POST /api/tasks with no model_tier -> task in DB has model_tier set to small, standard, or strong (not null)
2. POST /api/tasks with model_tier='strong' -> task keeps model_tier='strong', not overwritten
3. Task with project_id='lobs-server-backend' (contains 'lobs-server') -> model_tier is 'standard' or 'strong', never 'small'
4. When LM Studio is unreachable (mock or down), task creation still succeeds with model_tier='standard'
5. Task creation API response time stays under 5s even with LM Studio call
6. Tests cover all four branches (existing tier, project minimum, LLM classify, fallback)
7. No imports of ModelChooser inside task_tier.py (avoids circular dependency)

title: Add explicit tier short-circuit to ModelChooser
context:
ModelChooser.choose() currently always calls decide_models() which uses agent_type + complexity heuristics to pick a tier. Now that task.model_tier is set at creation time, we should use it directly when present.

In app/orchestrator/model_chooser.py, in ModelChooser.choose(), add this logic BEFORE the call to decide_models():

From app/orchestrator.model_router import TIER_ORDER

explicit_tier = (task or {}).get('model_tier')
if explicit_tier and explicit_tier in TIER_ORDER:
    # Use explicit tier from task — bypass heuristics
    tier_candidates = list(tiers.get(explicit_tier) or [])
    # Also prepend local models for this tier if discovered
    # (already handled above via discover_ollama/lmstudio prepend)
    if tier_candidates:
        candidates = tier_candidates[:]
        # Continue to budget guards, health ranking, routing policy as normal
        # Build minimal audit dict for this path
        audit_base = {'tier_source': 'explicit', 'tier': explicit_tier, 'tier_models': tiers}
        # ... run rest of choose() with candidates instead of decision.models
    else:
        # Tier list empty in DB — fall back to decide_models
        audit_base = {'tier_source': 'heuristic_fallback', 'reason': 'explicit tier list empty'}
        decision = decide_models(...)
else:
    audit_base = {'tier_source': 'heuristic'}
    decision = decide_models(...)

The cleanest approach is to refactor the body after decide_models() into a helper that accepts (candidates, tiers, complexity, criticality) and call it from both branches.

IMPORTANT: Keep all budget guardrails, health ranking, and routing policy logic intact. The short-circuit only affects HOW candidates is initially populated — not what happens after.
acceptance:
1. Task with model_tier='small' -> ModelChooser returns a model from MODEL_ROUTER_TIER_SMALL list (set this in DB settings)
2. Task with model_tier='strong' -> ModelChooser returns a model from MODEL_ROUTER_TIER_STRONG list
3. Task with model_tier=None -> ModelChooser falls back to decide_models() heuristics (existing behavior)
4. Task with model_tier='small' but MODEL_ROUTER_TIER_SMALL is empty in DB -> falls back to decide_models()
5. ModelChoice.audit contains 'tier_source' key ('explicit' or 'heuristic' or 'heuristic_fallback')
6. Budget guards still fire — a task with tier='strong' but lane over budget still gets downgraded
7. Existing ModelChooser tests still pass
8. No LLM calls happen inside ModelChooser.choose() for tier resolution
